questions.scenario: A teammate says App Service and Container Apps are both managed, so the operational work is identical. Which answer is most accurate?

options.text: They are identical once the code reaches Azure.
App Service starts from a web app runtime, while Container Apps starts from a container image and revision model. Both are managed, but the deployment and debugging evidence differs.
Container Apps removes the need for health checks because Azure controls the replicas.
App Service cannot use managed identity, so it needs copied secrets.

questions.explanation: The course compares what Azure manages with what the team still needs to understand. Managed does not mean identical evidence or identical release behavior.

questions.scenario: The team wants the least server maintenance for a small queue job that sends receipts after orders. It should run when events arrive and stay idle otherwise. Which compute choice best fits the article?

options.text: Azure Functions, because the work starts from an event.
Azure Virtual Machines, because queue jobs need a full operating system.
Azure App Service, because all Node.js code should run as a web app.
Azure Container Registry, because it stores the job image.

questions.explanation: A function app is a natural first choice when the job starts from an event and does not need to behave like an always-on API.

questions.scenario: Which questions belong in a practical runtime decision record before the team chooses a compute service?

options.text: Does the workload need to answer user traffic continuously, react to events, or run as a controlled machine?
What logs, health checks, deployment model, and rollback path will the team use?
Can every workload use the same resource type so the diagram is shorter?
What configuration and identity will the runtime need after it starts?

questions.explanation: A useful decision record describes the job, evidence, and operating path. Fewer boxes on a diagram are helpful only when they still match the workload.
